sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 30-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of right flank pain radiating to the groin, which worsens with increased fluid intake and physical activity.
The pain is described as a 4 out of 10, but can increase to a 4 out of 10 when uncontrolled.
The patient reports no nausea, vomiting, or fever.
He has a history of kidney stones, having passed some on his own and requiring surgery twice.
His family history is significant for kidney stones.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of kidney stones, with previous episodes of passing stones on his own and requiring surgery.
His family history is also significant for kidney stones.
Review of systems: The patient denies any additional symptoms in other organs.
Current medications: The patient is currently taking ibuprofen for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is slightly elevated, likely due to pain.
Physical exam: The physical exam reveals no tenderness to palpation of the abdomen, but there is CVA tenderness on the right side, consistent with the location of the kidney stone.
Test Results: A CT scan showed a 0.5 cm stone located on the proximal right ureter, with no evidence of high flow nephrosis or obstruction.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a confirmed diagnosis of a 0.5 cm kidney stone located on the proximal right ureter.
Problem list: Kidney stone; confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen to continue managing the patient's pain.
I also prescribed tramadol 50 mg as needed for breakthrough pain.
I ordered a BMP, urinalysis, and urine culture to rule out infection.
The patient is to continue using a strainer when urinating to attempt to catch the stone.
I will see the patient in 1-2 weeks to reassess symptoms and discuss further treatment options, including lithotripsy.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in 1-2 weeks to reassess symptoms and discuss further treatment options.
